sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 156, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Target (48,33) collected (W). But previously collected (60,27) reset to blue. Pattern: only current position stays collected, others reset. Hypothesis: switching entities with SPACE preserves collected targets. Need to test this.
- Goal model: Collect all 9 targets (3r, 3b, 3o) using 3 entities. Switching entities may lock in collections.
- Plan: Test SPACE switch to see if (48,33) stays W. If yes, collect all blue targets, switch, collect orange, switch, collect red.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 135 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
KEY FINDING: After pressing SPACE, the target at (48, 33) reset from W back to b (blue). This means switching entities does NOT preserve collected targets.
Also, the entities changed:
- Before: B@28,37, b@42,48, p@63,31
- After: B@45,33, p@63,20, w@63,52
Wait, the entity colors changed too:
- Before: B (blue cross), b (blue), p (purple)
- After: B (blue cross), p (purple), w (white/light gray?)
The cursor is now at (42, 48), which was the position of the "b" entity before. So SPACE switched to the entity at (42, 48).
But the entity colors are different now. Let me look at the hashes to track entities:
- Before: B hash=8c13a2d2, b hash=671d83f0, p hash=3b94f6c9
- After: Need to check hashes
Actually, I didn't print the hashes. Let me check.
But more importantly, I see that:
1. Targets reset when I move away from them
2. Targets also reset when I switch entities
This means the collection mechanic is different from what I thought. Maybe the goal is not to collect targets permanently, but to visit them all in a specific sequence or pattern.
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the targets:
- r@[3, 21], r@[9, 27], r@[12, 12] - 3 red targets
- b@[36, 27], b@[48, 15], b@[48, 33], b@[60, 27] - 4 blue targets
- O@[39, 9], O@[42, 24], O@[57, 9] - 3 orange targets
That's 10 targets total (3 red, 4 blue, 3 orange).
But earlier I thought there were only 3 blue targets. Let me recount:
- b@[36, 27], b@[48, 15], b@[48, 33], b@[60, 27] - yes, 4 blue targets
And the entities are:
- B (blue cross) - collects blue targets?
- b (orange X?) - collects orange targets?
- p (red diamond) - collects red targets?
Wait, the entity colors don't match the target colors. The blue cross (B) might collect blue (b) targets, the orange X might collect orange (O) targets, and the red diamond (p) might collect red (r) targets.
But the collection mechanic seems to only work temporarily. When I move away or switch entities, the targets reset.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have all targets collected SIMULTANEOUSLY at the end. This would require:
1. Position all three entities on their respective targets simultaneously
2. But I can only control one entity at a time
Or maybe the goal is different. Let me look at the level completion condition.
Actually, I recall from earlier that when I collected a target, it turned WHITE. And when I moved away, it reset. This suggests the targets are "active" only when an entity is on them.
Maybe the goal is to have all three entities on their respective targets at the same time. This would require:
1. Position entity 1 on target 1
2. Switch to entity 2, position on target 2
3. Switch to entity 3, position on target 3
4. But when I switch, the previous entity's target resets
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe the entities don't move when I switch. Let me check:
- Before SPACE: cursor at (48, 33), entities at B@28,37, b@42,48, p@63,31
- After SPACE: cursor at (42, 48), entities at B@45,33, p@63,20, w@63,52
The entity positions changed! The blue cross moved from (28, 37) to (45, 33), the red diamond moved from (63, 31) to (63, 20), and there's a new entity w@63,52.
Wait, this is confusing. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the entities moved because I was controlling the blue cross and moved it around. The blue cross was at (48, 33) when I pressed SPACE. After SPACE, the blue cross is at (45, 33), which is close to (48, 33). So the blue cross didn't move much.
But the red diamond moved from (63, 31) to (63, 20), which is a significant change. And there's a new entity w@63,52.
I think the entity tracking is getting confused. Let me just focus on the current state and try to understand the game mechanics better.
Current state:
- Cursor at (42, 48)
- Entities: B@45,33, p@63,20, w@63,52
- Targets: 3 red, 4 blue, 3 orange (10 total)
The cursor is at (42, 48), which is near the orange X entity. Let me check which entity I'm controlling.
Actually, looking at the image, the cursor (white pixel) is on the red diamond entity. The red diamond is in the center-right area.
Let me try to understand the entity-color mapping:
- B (blue) - blue cross
- p (purple) - red diamond (but color is purple, not red)
- w (white/light gray) - orange X (but color is white, not orange)
The entity colors don't match the visual colors. The segmentation uses ARC color codes, which might not match the visual appearance.
Let me just try to collect targets with the current entity and see what happens.
Current cursor: (42, 48)
Closest target: O@[42, 24] (orange)
From (42, 48) to (42, 24): LEFT 8 (48->24)
Let me try this and see if the orange target gets collected.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: CRITICAL - SPACE switch caused target (48,33) to reset from W to b. Collections do NOT persist across entity switches. Entities moved: B@45,33, p@63,20, w@63,52. Cursor now at (42,48) on a different entity.
Goal model: Need to understand collection mechanic better. Targets only stay collected while entity is on them.
Plan: Test collecting an orange target with current entity from (42,48). Target O@[42,24] is LEFT 8 away.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor: (42, 48)
# Closest target: O@[42, 24] (same row, LEFT 8)
# Let me collect it and check

action(['LEFT']*8)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"Cursor: {n['boundary'][0]}")

print("Targets:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        for cid in n['children']:
            cn = nodes[cid]
            print(f"  {cn['color']}@{cn['boundary'][0]}")

print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")

# Check entities
print("\nEntities:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] > 20 and n['pixels'] < 500:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, hash={n['hash'][:8]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  p: center=(63, 18), hash=5f83bfa7
  w: center=(63, 50), hash=cdce2941
